01a_quotes.title: Experimenting with Generative AI: Does ChatGPT Really Increase Everyone's Productivity?

study_design: We conducted an experiment in which economics students were asked to perform writing analysis tasks in a non-English language (specifically, Thai) and math & data analysis tasks using a less frequently used programming package (specifically, Stata).
This study involved 121 college economics students from Chulalongkorn University and Thammasat University in Thailand.

Participants began with two sessions of writing analysis tasks, comparing performance with and without ChatGPT. They then proceeded to two sessions of math & data analysis tasks, again with and without ChatGPT.
In all sessions, they had access to a browser/internet but were not allowed to use other Large Language Model (LLM) platforms.
To accurately assess the impact of ChatGPT, we prepared two sets of tasks (Set A and Set B) of equal difficulty for both writing analysis and math & data analysis.
To eliminate any bias from the difference in problem sets or the order of using ChatGPT, participants were randomly divided into four groups as detailed in Table 1.
We assessed performance using two metrics: a score out of 10 (averaged from the assessments of three experts) and the time required to complete tasks.
Each task session had a 20-minute limit.
Participants could submit their work before this time, but were required to submit whatever they had at the 20-minute mark.

Following all sessions, participants completed a questionnaire detailing their academic background (including GPAX and econometrics grade) and self-assessed their reading, writing, math, and digital skills on a scale from 1 to 5.
task: The writing analysis tasks encompassed brainstorming, reading and providing feedback on texts, composing tweets, and summarizing documents.
The math & data analysis tasks included coding for data visualization, variable generation, conducting regression analyses, hypothesis testing, and equation derivation.
population: This study involved 121 college economics students from Chulalongkorn University and Thammasat University in Thailand.
Most them are in their 3rd and 4th years.
Their average Grade Point Average (GPAX) is 3.32, with an average econometrics grade of 3.00.
About 36% of these students are male.
They rated their abilities in reading, writing, math, and digital skills on a scale from 1 to 5 (where 5 is the highest), with average scores of 3.46, 3.07, 3.26, and 3.20, respectively.
Notably, approximately 6.6% of the participants reported never having used ChatGPT.
arms: Group 1 started with Set A of writing analysis without ChatGPT, followed by Set B with ChatGPT, then proceeded to Set A of math & data analysis without ChatGPT, and finished with Set B with ChatGPT.
outcome_definitions: We assessed performance using two metrics: a score out of 10 (averaged from the assessments of three experts) and the time required to complete tasks.
Score_Diff refers to the difference in scores a student achieved when using ChatGPT versus not using ChatGPT.
'Score Diff' is defined as the score a student achieved when using ChatGPT minus the score achieved when not using ChatGPT.
'Time Diff' is defined as the time taken when not using ChatGPT minus the time taken when using ChatGPT.
statistical_results: On average, the score with ChatGPT is higher than the score without ChatGPT by 0.43 (out of a total possible score of 10), while the time to complete tasks with ChatGPT is shorter than without by 1.73 minutes.
Despite the overall improvement in scores with ChatGPT, 41 out of 121 students (approximately 34%) did not see improved outcomes.
On average, scores with ChatGPT are higher than those without ChatGPT by 1.65 (out of a total score of 10), while the time taken with ChatGPT is shorter than without by 0.99 minutes.
Despite the general improvement in scores with ChatGPT, 51 out of 121 students (approximately 42%) did not perform better.

The Econo variable is consistently negative and significant across all specifications at the 10% level, suggesting that students with higher econometrics grades performed worse when using ChatGPT, with scores approximately 0.54 to 0.57 points lower.

The Digital skills variable is positive and significant at the 10% level in all models, except for Column 6, implying that students with higher digital skills scores performed better when using ChatGPT, with an approximate increase of 0.70 to 0.73 points in their scores.
The Econo variable is negative and significant at the 5% level across all specifications, indicating that students with higher econometrics grades performed worse when using ChatGPT.

The Digital Skills variable is positive and significant at the 5% level in all specifications, demonstrating that students with superior digital skills achieved better results with the use of ChatGPT.
In the analysis of writing tasks, presented in Columns 1-4, the Econo variable consistently appears negative and significant at the 5% level across all specifications.

Additionally, the Digital Skills variable is positive and significant at the 10% level in all specifications, indicating that students with superior digital skills achieve better results with ChatGPT.

For the math & data analysis tasks, detailed in Columns 5-8, the Digital Skills variable again shows a positive and significant relationship, at levels ranging from 5% to 10%, across all specifications.
In Panel A, it can be observed that, on average, participants achieved higher scores when permitted to use ChatGPT for both writing analysis and math & data analysis tasks.
Panel B illustrated that, on average, participants completed tasks, both writing analysis and math & data analysis, more quickly when using ChatGPT.
In testing for differences in means, only the Econometrics grade showed statistical significance at the 10% level.
However, none of the t-tests reached statistical significance.
ambiguity_or_caveats: However, there is still limited literature regarding ChatGPT's performance in areas where its capabilities could still be further enhanced.
However, a detailed examination reveals that 34% of participants saw no improvement in writing analysis tasks, and 42% did not improve in math & data analysis tasks when employing ChatGPT.
Further investigation indicated that higher-ability students, as proxied by their econometrics grades, were the ones who performed worse in writing analysis tasks when using ChatGPT.
This discrepancy underscores the importance of investigating the characteristics that differentiate students who benefited from ChatGPT from those who did not.
Interestingly, students who did not benefit from ChatGPT generally have higher GPAX and econometrics grades.

Figure 2 explores this issue from a distributional angle, contrasting students who improved with ChatGPT against those who did not. Panel B shows that the distribution of econometrics grades among students who did not benefit from ChatGPT tends to lie to the right when compared to their counterparts. Patterns GPAX and for other skills, however, remain inconclusive.
Further observation reveals that, on average, students who did not improve with ChatGPT tend to have higher GPAX, econometrics grades, and self-assessed reading and writing skills.
However, none of the t-tests reached statistical significance.
Figure 3 explores this issue through the distribution of scores, but the patterns remain inconclusive.

Our hypothesis is that econometrics grades might serve as a more accurate measure of economics students' abilities compared to GPAX, which encompasses a broader range of subjects, including those not directly related to economics (e.g., General Education).
Additionally, the ChatExp variable, a proxy of 'ChatGPT proficiency' determined by how oftern each student uses ChatGPT per week, was never significant our models.

Our hypothesis is that general digital competencies may enable students to more effectively engage with any digital or technical program, including ChatGPT, regardless of their frequency of ChatGPT usage per week.

01a_quotes.notes: This is an experimental study with a within-subjects design comparing ChatGPT use vs. no ChatGPT use on writing analysis tasks in Thai and math/data analysis tasks in Stata. The paper includes main OLS regression results (Table 6) and supplemental analyses using Linear Probability Model (Table 7) and Logistic regression (Table 8). Results are reported separately for writing analysis and math & data analysis tasks.

01_extraction.title: Experimenting with Generative AI: Does ChatGPT Really Increase Everyone's Productivity?
01_extraction.study_design_description: An experiment with 121 college economics students from Chulalongkorn University and Thammasat University in Thailand. Participants completed two sessions of writing analysis tasks (in Thai) and two sessions of math & data analysis tasks (in Stata), each comparing performance with and without ChatGPT access. To eliminate bias from problem sets and task order, participants were randomly divided into four groups. Performance was assessed using expert-scored ratings (out of 10) and time to completion, with each task session limited to 20 minutes.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Writing analysis tasks encompassing brainstorming, reading and providing feedback on texts, composing tweets, and summarizing documents; and math & data analysis tasks including coding for data visualization, variable generation, conducting regression analyses, hypothesis testing, and equation derivation.
01_extraction.population_description: 121 college economics students from Chulalongkorn University and Thammasat University in Thailand, mostly in their 3rd and 4th years. Average GPAX of 3.32 and average econometrics grade of 3.00. Approximately 36% male. Self-assessed reading, writing, math, and digital skills averaged 3.46, 3.07, 3.26, and 3.20 respectively on a 1-5 scale. Approximately 6.6% had never used ChatGPT.

arms.description: Started with Set A of writing analysis without ChatGPT, followed by Set B with ChatGPT, then proceeded to Set A of math & data analysis without ChatGPT, and finished with Set B with ChatGPT.

outcomes.outcome_description: Score out of 10, averaged from assessments of three experts, comparing performance when using ChatGPT versus not using ChatGPT.
Time required to complete tasks, comparing time taken when using ChatGPT versus not using ChatGPT.
Score difference in writing analysis tasks when using ChatGPT versus not using ChatGPT.
Time difference in writing analysis tasks when not using ChatGPT minus time when using ChatGPT.
Score difference in math & data analysis tasks when using ChatGPT versus not using ChatGPT.
Time difference in math & data analysis tasks when not using ChatGPT minus time when using ChatGPT.

the time to complete tasks with ChatGPT is shorter than without by 1.73 minutes

reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: The Digital skills variable is positive and significant at the 10% level in all models, except for Column 6, implying that students with higher digital skills scores performed better when using ChatGPT, with an approximate increase of 0.70 to 0.73 points in their scores.
The Econo variable is negative and significant at the 5% level across all specifications, indicating that students with higher econometrics grades performed worse when using ChatGPT.

The Digital Skills variable is positive and significant at the 5% level in all specifications, demonstrating that students with superior digital skills achieved better results with the use of ChatGPT.
In the analysis of writing tasks, presented in Columns 1-4, the Econo variable consistently appears negative and significant at the 5% level across all specifications.

Additionally, the Digital Skills variable is positive and significant at the 10% level in all specifications, indicating that students with superior digital skills achieve better results with ChatGPT.

For the math & data analysis tasks, detailed in Columns 5-8, the Digital Skills variable again shows a positive and significant relationship, at levels ranging from 5% to 10%, across all specifications.
In Panel A, it can be observed that, on average, participants achieved higher scores when permitted to use ChatGPT for both writing analysis and math & data analysis tasks.
Panel B illustrated that, on average, participants completed tasks, both writing analysis and math & data analysis, more quickly when using ChatGPT.

01_extraction.notes: This is a within-subjects experimental design where all 121 participants completed both ChatGPT and no-ChatGPT conditions across writing analysis and math & data analysis tasks. The four groups (n=21, 34, 44, 22) represent different task orderings to control for order effects. The paper reports that 34% of participants (41/121) did not improve in writing analysis tasks and 42% (51/121) did not improve in math & data analysis tasks when using ChatGPT. Regression results show that econometrics grade (a proxy for ability) is negatively associated with ChatGPT benefit, while digital skills are positively associated. The paper notes that ChatGPT experience (frequency of use per week) was never significant in models. Statistical significance testing for differences in means showed only econometrics grade reached significance at the 10% level; however, the quote states "none of the t-tests reached statistical significance," which appears to refer to a specific set of comparisons (possibly baseline characteristics). Detailed regression coefficients and standard errors are referenced as being in Tables 6-8 but are not provided in the quotes.

02_method.rationale: The study explicitly states that participants 'completed two sessions of writing analysis tasks... and two sessions of math & data analysis tasks, each comparing performance with and without ChatGPT access.' This indicates the same participants performed tasks both with and without AI assistance, which is the defining characteristic of a within-subject design.

per_outcome.operationalization: Score out of 10 averaged across three expert assessments of task performance
Time in minutes required to complete tasks, measured from start to completion
Difference in expert-assessed scores (out of 10) for writing analysis tasks between conditions
Difference in minutes to complete writing analysis tasks between conditions
Difference in expert-assessed scores (out of 10) for math and data analysis tasks between conditions
Difference in minutes to complete math and data analysis tasks between conditions

04_onet.rationale: The participants performed diverse analytical tasks including 'reading and providing feedback on texts,' 'conducting regression analyses, hypothesis testing,' 'coding for data visualization,' and 'summarizing documents.' These activities collectively represent information processing work—gathering, organizing, analyzing, and interpreting data and text. While the students are economics majors, the study measures their performance on generalized analytical work activities rather than occupation-specific consulting or research roles. Processing Information best captures this breadth of analytical tasks.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Within-subject crossover design where all 121 participants completed writing analysis tasks both with and without ChatGPT. This is the only AI-assisted condition available for the writing analysis tasks, which the paper emphasizes as a primary productivity measure.
llm_extracted.notes: Table 3 Panel B provides the complete statistics for time spent on writing analysis tasks. Without ChatGPT: Mean=19.19 minutes, SD=1.62, Min=9.00, Max=20.00. With ChatGPT: Mean=17.46 minutes, SD=3.09, Min=7.00, Max=20.00. N=121 for both conditions (within-subject design). Table 4 Panel A confirms the time difference: 'Time Diff = No ChatGPT - With ChatGPT' has Mean=1.73, SD=3.43. The mean difference of -1.73 minutes (M_ai - M_human = 17.46 - 19.19) indicates participants completed tasks faster with ChatGPT. Time is the outcome, so smaller is better.
source_quotes: Table 3 Panel B: Time Spent (Maximum is 20 Minutes) - Writing Analysis - No ChatGPT: Obs=121, Mean=19.19, Std. Dev.=1.62, Min=9.00, Max=20.00
Table 3 Panel B: Writing Analysis - With ChatGPT: Obs=121, Mean=17.46, Std. Dev.=3.09, Min=7.00, Max=20.00
Table 4 Panel A: Time Diff = No ChatGPT - With ChatGPT: Obs=121, Mean=1.73, Std. Dev.=3.43, Min=-9.00, Max=13.00
Panel B illustrated that, on average, participants completed tasks, both writing analysis and math & data analysis, more quickly when using ChatGPT.

computed.notes: Table 3 Panel B provides the complete statistics for time spent on writing analysis tasks. Without ChatGPT: Mean=19.19 minutes, SD=1.62, Min=9.00, Max=20.00. With ChatGPT: Mean=17.46 minutes, SD=3.09, Min=7.00, Max=20.00. N=121 for both conditions (within-subject design). Table 4 Panel A confirms the time difference: 'Time Diff = No ChatGPT - With ChatGPT' has Mean=1.73, SD=3.43. The mean difference of -1.73 minutes (M_ai - M_human = 17.46 - 19.19) indicates participants completed tasks faster with ChatGPT. Time is the outcome, so smaller is better.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: This is a within-subjects crossover design where all 121 participants completed writing analysis tasks both with and without ChatGPT. The comparison is between the 'with ChatGPT' and 'without ChatGPT' conditions completed by the same participants.
llm_extracted.notes: Within-subjects crossover design with n=121. The prior pass missed the SD of difference scores, which is reported in Table 4, Panel A as 1.98. Individual condition means and SDs are reported in Table 3, Panel A: Writing Analysis - No ChatGPT (M=5.33, SD=1.47) and Writing Analysis - With ChatGPT (M=5.75, SD=1.47). The mean difference is 0.43 points (out of 10). With the SD of differences now available, Hedges' g can be computed for this paired design.
source_quotes: Score Diff = With ChatGPT - No ChatGPT 121 0.43 1.98 -5.00 5.17
On average, the score with ChatGPT is higher than the score without ChatGPT by 0.43 (out of a total possible score of 10)

computed.notes: Within-subjects crossover design with n=121. The prior pass missed the SD of difference scores, which is reported in Table 4, Panel A as 1.98. Individual condition means and SDs are reported in Table 3, Panel A: Writing Analysis - No ChatGPT (M=5.33, SD=1.47) and Writing Analysis - With ChatGPT (M=5.75, SD=1.47). The mean difference is 0.43 points (out of 10). With the SD of differences now available, Hedges' g can be computed for this paired design.
